p.em: This document demonstrates CSS page-break control, orphans/widows, and differentiated first-page headers.

main.p: The boxes below have page-break-inside: avoid applied. Instead of being split across pages, each box will be moved entirely to the next page if it doesn't fit.

div.p: This entire card is kept together on one page. The layout engine checks if the box fits in the remaining space. If it doesn't, the box is moved to a fresh page rather than being split.
This prevents ugly breaks in the middle of visually grouped content like cards, alerts, or callout boxes.
Another avoid-break box. Notice how no card is ever cut in half across a page boundary. This is critical for professional document output.
Without this feature, a box's top half might appear at the bottom of one page and the bottom half at the top of the next — something that looks terrible in printed documents.

main.p: Here is some filler text to push content down the page and demonstrate page breaks happening naturally between elements.
The layout engine intelligently decides where to break. It respects the CSS cascade, meaning you can set page-break-inside: avoid on specific classes without affecting the rest of the document.
This is paragraph three of additional content. We need enough text to push the layout across the page boundary so the next section demonstrates forced breaks.
Additional paragraph to extend the content area. The pagination engine processes each direct child of the body element sequentially, checking overflow conditions at every step.
More content to fill the page. This is important because we want to see how the engine handles the transition from page 1 to page 2, especially with the differentiated headers.
Notice that page 1 has the large title header, while page 2 shows the smaller continuation header. This is the first-header/first-footer feature in action.

div.p: This card uses page-break-inside: avoid. If it can't fit at the bottom of the current page, it moves entirely to the next one.
Another card that stays together. This demonstrates the feature working with multiple consecutive avoid-break elements.
Third card in the sequence. Each one independently checks whether it fits on the current page.
Fourth card. At this point, we should be seeing cards flowing across pages while maintaining their integrity.
Fifth and final card in this demonstration section. The pagination algorithm handles each one cleanly.
This section has page-break-before: always applied via CSS. Regardless of how much space remains on the previous page, this section starts on a fresh page.
This is essential for documents like legal contracts, where each section must begin on its own page, or reports where chapters need clear separation.
The table below contains many rows to demonstrate how tables flow across pages. Notice the clean page transitions.

The paragraphs in this document use orphans: 3; widows: 3; which means the layout engine ensures at least 3 lines appear at the bottom of a page before a break, and at least 3 lines appear at the top of the next page after a break.
This prevents the common typographical error of having a single lonely line stranded at the top or bottom of a page, which looks unprofessional in printed documents.

Professional typesetting has enforced orphan and widow rules for centuries. The term "orphan" refers to the first line of a paragraph stranded alone at the bottom of a page, while "widow" refers to the last line stranded at the top of the next page. Both are considered bad practice in professional publishing.
By setting these CSS properties, you get automatic protection against these typographical issues without having to manually adjust your content. The layout engine handles it during pagination.
This is the final section of the M3 pagination demo. Thank you for exploring the advanced pagination features of pdfml!
